data.title: Tech giants go prime time in the fight for China’s biggest audience
data.description:
China’s biggest tech groups and a clutch of fast-rising consumer electronics brands are battling for coveted sponsorship slots at the Spring Festival Gala, as intensifying competition in artificial intelligence turns the country’s most watched television broadcast into a rare nationwide marketing battleground.
Major players including ByteDance, Tencent Holdings and Alibaba Group Holding had all held discussions with state broadcaster China Central Television (CCTV) over high-stakes partnership packages, according to people familiar with the matter.
ByteDance’s cloud computing arm, Volcano Engine, had previously been reported as the gala’s exclusive AI cloud partner, while the company’s AI chatbot app Doubao was expected to introduce new interactive features during the broadcast, Chinese business outlet LatePost reported last week.
However, the final sponsorship structure had yet to be settled, one person familiar with the talks said, as ByteDance had pushed for exclusive AI-related terms that would effectively bar the mention or use of rival cloud and AI software providers – a demand that has complicated negotiations.
The fight is about more than logos: the gala has a track record of turning product features into mass adoption moments.
Shanghai-based robotics start-up Agibot had also been reported as bidding for a slot. Unitree Robotics secured a partnership for the previous show in January, one person said, adding that the deal cost about 100 million yuan (US$14 million). The company made headlines at that gala performance when six humanoid robots performed a folk dance.
ByteDance declined to comment, while Tencent and Alibaba, which owns the Post, did not respond to a request for comment.
As a result, a number of emerging consumer electronics brands – less constrained by exclusivity rules – were also seeking exposure during the broadcast. These included Hangzhou-based smart glasses maker Rokid and home-appliance brand Dreame, best known for its robotic vacuum cleaners, according to people familiar with the matter.
None of the companies immediately responded to requests for comment on Friday.
The 2026 Spring Festival Gala, scheduled for February 16 on the eve of the Lunar New Year, had drawn aggressive bids from companies aligned with China’s so-called “new productive forces”, one person said, as competition in cloud services and AI applications intensified.
Products such as Tencent’s Yuanbao and Alibaba’s Qwen were racing to build user bases in an increasingly crowded market.

Chinese technology outlet 36Kr reported this week that Doubao’s daily active users had surpassed 100 million. Earlier data from QuestMobile showed the app’s monthly active users reached 172 million in September, placing it ahead of DeepSeek at 145 million and Tencent’s Yuanbao at 33 million.
Meanwhile, analytics firm Xsignal said Qwen reached about 44.3 million monthly active users within a month of its public beta launch on November 17.
Few marketing opportunities in China rival the Spring Festival Gala, which has evolved from a cultural fixture into a high-stakes proving ground for market dominance.
According to CCTV, the 2025 Year of the Snake gala drew a cumulative audience of 16.8 billion views across all media channels, with a live television viewing share of 78.88 per cent. A viral performance by humanoid robots from Unitree during the show propelled the Hangzhou-based start-up into the national spotlight overnight.
Historically, the gala has served both as a powerful advertising platform and as a form of unofficial endorsement. Sponsors have ranged from watchmakers, liquor brands and home appliance companies in earlier decades to internet giants in more recent years, reflecting shifts in China’s industrial priorities.
In 2015, WeChat became the gala’s exclusive interactive platform, popularising mobile payments through a virtual red-envelope campaign that challenged the dominance of Alipay.
Alipay later followed with its own gamified promotions, while Alibaba went on to secure title sponsorships in subsequent years. Baidu, as well as short-video platforms Kuaishou and Douyin, have also taken turns sponsoring the broadcast.
In 2025, Alibaba, RedNote and Bilibili were among the gala’s official partners across different categories.

data.title: China’s mega expressway tunnel opens in Xinjiang, halves north-south travel time
data.description:
The world’s longest expressway tunnel has opened to traffic in the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region, capping five years of construction on a project designed to increase China’s links with Central Asia.
The 22.13km (13.75-mile) Tianshan Shengli Tunnel, the centrepiece of the Urumqi-Yuli Expressway, officially opened on Friday, creating a 20-minute drive through the Tianshan Mountains that divide northern and southern Xinjiang.
The tunnel halves the travel time between the regional capital Urumqi and the city of Korla from over seven hours to around 3½ hours, according to state media.
Song Hailiang, chairman of China Communications Construction Company (CCCC), told state broadcaster CCTV that the tunnel “set two world records”.
“It is the longest expressway tunnel in the world and has the deepest vertical shaft for a highway tunnel,” Song said.
Work started on the expressway in April 2020 and engineers had to confront a range of challenges from the terrain and environment.


The tunnel cuts a path through the mountains at nearly 3,000 metres (9,842 feet) above sea level and crews working on the project faced extreme cold, with temperatures dropping as low as minus 42 degrees Celsius (minus 43.6 Fahrenheit).
The tunnel’s deepest point is 1,112 metres below the altitude at the entry and shafts had to be dug over 700 metres below the surface to take equipment and workers to and from the construction face.
Miao Baodong, chief engineer of CCCC’s Xinjiang Transport Investment and Development arm, told CCTV that the project would have taken at least a decade using traditional tunnelling methods.
However, the engineers employed a novel “three tunnels plus four shafts” strategy, using a tunnel boring machine designed for hard rock.
The result is a fast link that is expected to enable the flow of more resources throughout the autonomous region, including energy and manufactured goods in the north to agricultural products from the south, according to officials.


The expressway is also expected to strengthen links with Central and South Asia under the Belt and Road Initiative, serving as a “strategic bridgehead for opening up to the West”, according to the CCTV report.
Xinjiang borders eight countries, including Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Pakistan, making it a hub for China’s regional and international trade.
Guo Sheng, deputy director of Xinjiang’s transport department, said the expressway connected the country to various economic corridors, underscoring Xinjiang’s “strategic role as a pivotal hub” for “dual circulation”.
Dual circulation is a national policy to strengthen domestic economic integration and foreign trade, in part through overland corridors.
However, Xinjiang has been a source of friction between China and the West over the years, with the United States and European Union imposing sanctions and export restrictions in response to allegations of human rights violations – charges Beijing has repeatedly denied.
China is also building a 1,980km railway connecting Hotan in Xinjiang to Lhasa in the Tibet autonomous region, another ambitious, costly “project of a century”.
It also presents extreme engineering challenges and is expected to boost regional integration, transport and national security.

data.title: Chinese diving star Chen Yuxi says she has received threats, denies she ‘has connections’
data.description:
Chen Yuxi, the 20-year-old Olympic diving champion, has opened up on social media about facing online abuse, being threatened with doxxing and people trying to create a rift within the Chinese diving team.
Chen also spoke out against claims that she has special “connections” in the sport through her family.
The video was released on her social media accounts on Wednesday after Chen was named World Aquatics’ 2025 Female Diver of the Year earlier this month.
Chen won the accolade after a return to form following a difficult period in the wake of the Paris Olympics last year.
In Paris, she won gold in the 10m synchronised platform with Quan Hongchan. She also won silver in the individual 10m platform event, but faced intense public pressure and injuries afterwards and had to deal with online abuse.

At the Diving World Cup Super Final in Beijing in May and at the Singapore World Championships in July, Chen won gold in the women’s 10m platform, synchronised 10m and mixed team events.
But, Chen said in the tearful 10-minute video interview, “this year has not been easy”.
“After the Paris Olympics, I never thought I could stand on the 10-metre platform again. The pressure made me want to escape,” she recalled.
In the interview, the reporter said Chen has been subjected to among the longest and most intense episodes of online harassment of any Chinese athlete.
Chen also spoke about a doxxing incident earlier in her career.
Doxxing is the act of publicly providing personally identifiable information about an individual or organisation, usually via the internet and without their consent.
“On the night before one of my individual finals, someone sent me a message listing all my personal details, including my ID number and phone number,” Chen said.
“I didn’t know what it meant and was very scared, so I reported it to the team immediately.”
In September, China’s Ministry of Public Security reported 10 cases of cybercrime and solved a case of online abuse against a Shanghai diver. According to the ministry, in May, several netizens insulted and defamed a diver and faced criminal penalties – it was revealed that Chen was the victim.
Chen is often described in media as coming from a sporting family: her grandfather was a well-known gymnast, her father is a senior gymnastics coach at a youth sports school in Shanghai, and her mother is an associate professor at Shanghai University of Sport.
This background has led to accusations that she “has connections”.
Chen said with a hint of frustration that if she performed well, people accused her of benefiting from relationships with referees, but if her results were not good, she was criticised for lacking ability.
“No matter how I perform, there are always baseless rumours,” she said, smiling through tears. “People think I have a privileged background just because my parents are coaches.”
Chen has closed some comment sections on her social media, as she does not want to see negative remarks about herself, her coaches or those around her.”
Since the Tokyo Olympics, Chen and teammate Quan have won honours for China as a pair and have often produced high-level contests in individual events.
However, their rivalry has been exaggerated by some online abusers.
“Most comments are friendly, but there is a small group trying to create division, which is not good for Chinese diving,” Chen said. “We are all good teammates, and we are all competing for China.”

She said some people have tried to frame her and Quan as rivals in every context, whether it was competition, training or daily life.
But she said their relationship was “not something these comments can affect”, and described Quan as “a source of support”, adding that even if she did not perform well, Quan was always there.
“She [Quan] is also caught in the centre of public opinion. We both face difficulties, so I hope the public can be kinder to us.”
In November, at the National Games, Chen led from start to finish in the women’s 10m platform, winning her first solo National Games title.
“Time gives the answer,” she said. “Returning to competition has helped me grow a lot.”

data.title: Japan stabbing rampage leaves 14 injured at Yokohama rubber factory
data.description:
More than a dozen people were injured in a stabbing attack in a factory in central Japan during which an unspecified liquid was also sprayed, an emergency services official said on Friday.
“Fourteen people are subject to transport by emergency services,” said Tomoharu Sugiyama, a firefighting department official in the city of Mishima, in the Shizuoka region.
He said a call was received at about 4.30pm from a nearby rubber factory saying “five or six people were stabbed by someone” and that a “spray-like liquid” had also been used.
Japanese media, including public broadcaster NHK, reported that police had arrested a man on suspicion of attempted murder.
The seriousness of the injuries was unknown, although NHK said all victims remained conscious.
Sugiyama said at least six of the 14 victims had been sent to hospital in a fleet of ambulances. The exact nature of the injuries was also unclear.
The factory in Mishima is run by Yokohama Rubber Company, whose business includes manufacturing tyres for trucks and buses, according to its corporate website.
Violent crime is relatively rare in Japan, which has a low murder rate and some of the world’s toughest gun laws.
However, there are occasional stabbing attacks and even shootings, including the assassination of former prime minister Shinzo Abe in 2022.
A Japanese man was sentenced to death in October for a shooting and stabbing rampage that killed four people, including two police officers, in 2023.
A 43-year-old man was also charged with attempted murder in May over a knife attack at Tokyo’s Toda-mae metro station.

data.title: The Hong Kong films released in 2025 ranked from worst to best
data.description:
There were, ultimately, no miracles for Hong Kong cinema in 2025.
It was a regrettable year that began with reports of further cinema closures and ended in collective mourning after the Tai Po fire tragedy, which in turn pushed back the release of Avatar: Fire and Ash (now renamed Avatar 3) – previously earmarked as a saviour for cinema operators this Christmas.
The forecast downturn in film investment has been keenly felt; even several “urban myth” titles – the label jokingly given to high-profile projects wrapped years ago but left indefinitely, and often inexplicably, on the shelf – were summoned to fill the increasingly sparse slate.
While long-time followers of Hong Kong film were relieved to finally witness such fabled productions as Remember What I Forgot, Atonement, Sons of the Neon Night and Golden Boy on the big screen, the general public’s enthusiasm for local narratives appears to have cooled.

No releases in 2025 came remotely close to matching the record-breaking runs of A Guilty Conscience, Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In and The Last Dance in the previous two years, all three of which surpassed the HK$100 million (US$12 million) mark.
Indeed, this year’s numbers make for grim reading: fewer than 40 Hong Kong films opened in cinemas domestically, and the highest-grossing title, the animation Another World, was the only film to take in over HK$14 million at the local box office.
To illustrate the severity of the slump, this is the first time the local box office champion of any year has fallen below HK$20 million since Michael Hui Koon-man’s 1981 comedy blockbuster Security Unlimited, whose nearly HK$18 million box office was a record at the time.
One can only hope that Hong Kong cinema can rediscover its spark soon with the coming releases of the Lunar New Year comedy Night King, which reunites A Guilty Conscience director Jack Ng Wai-lun and star Dayo Wong Tze-wah, as well as the two much-anticipated “urban myth films” Back to the Past and Ciao UFO.

Once the glow of these nostalgic treasures fades, one wonders what new dreams will rise to fill the void.
Here we rank, from worst to best, the Hong Kong films released in the past 12 months that we have reviewed.
34. Ha Ha Ha Happy New Year

A goofy Lunar New Year comedy that is awkwardly framed around a premise taken straight out of a dark psychological sci-fi thriller, Ha Ha Ha Happy New Year is scattershot, often terrible, and as confused about what it really is as its temporarily amnesiac protagonist.
This Hong Kong-Malaysia co-production looks to be a passion project for Philip Keung Ho-man, who is both its producer and leading man.
Unfortunately, the film is so ill-served by its frivolous and tonally uneven screenplay that it barely registers as a nonsensical comedy, family melodrama or sci-fi suspense thriller – all of which it lays claim to being without ever coming close to succeeding. Read the full review
33. Once Upon a Lie

Irene Wan Pik-ha may have never come close to making the A-list of Hong Kong cinema, but the veteran actress could certainly never be faulted for not trying hard enough.
Her latest film, Once Upon a Lie, is a scenic but somewhat creepy romance that sees Wan’s character throw herself into the arms of an unsuspecting much younger man whom she also happens to be catfishing online.
This superficial first feature by director and co-screenwriter Stanley Liu Ming-yin duly takes shape as a May-December romance, even though it stays unconvincing to the end. Overall, it feels like this is but a romantic fantasy willed into existence by Wan, the self-made leading lady. Read the full review
32. Queen of Mahjong

In a match made in cinematic purgatory, Wong Jing’s exhaustive fascination with the gambling movie genre meets Patrick Kong Pak-leung’s brand of crass relationship drama in Queen of Mahjong.
It’s a rare opportunity for the two critically maligned filmmakers to share directing credits and their mutual embrace of sexism.
If you are a mahjong aficionado who also happens to be a decent human being, it might be very hard work to sit through all the rubbish in Queen of Mahjong to get to the few good bits. Read the full review
31. Burning Blood

Action thrillers set around drug cartels in Thailand’s Golden Triangle have been a popular recipe for Hong Kong-China co-productions in the past decade. Unfortunately, Burning Blood, Wilson Chin Kwok-wai’s final feature, is inferior to most of those films in every single department.
Its screenplay is filled with clichés but devoid of character development, the action is forgettable, the CGI effects are atrocious, the overall direction is shoddy, and even its lead actors, Jordan Chan Siu-chun and Michael Tse Tin-wah, fail to display the charm and charisma required to carry the unconvincing story. Read the full review
30. Measure in Love

A high-concept fantasy romance set in a human world that has been physically split into two, Measure in Love asks its audience to succumb to the supposedly poetic swooning of its star-crossed lovers – only to make it a very tall order indeed with a criminally underdeveloped set-up and an unfathomably illogical plot.
This dystopian fairy tale imagines a “gravity wall” separating humanity across two sides of the ocean. Angela Yuen Lai-lam and Greg Hsu Kuang-han make an attractive screen couple and Man Lim-chung’s art design does a wonderful job of transporting the viewers into the wasteland of Evergreen.
It is just a shame Measure in Love makes so little effort to be logical that only the biggest fans of its two leads are likely to merrily sit through the resulting piece of lyrical nonsense. Read the full review
29. Reborn

In Danny Pang Fat’s supernatural drama Reborn, Venus Wong Man-yik plays a young mother whose grief over her deceased son takes a bizarre form when she adopts a rag doll said to be possessed by the seven-year-old boy’s spirit, much to the chagrin of her husband (Eddie Cheung Siu-fai).
While the toy is meant to serve as a surrogate, the film it inhabits proves a poor substitute for genuine horror. Curiously unwilling to plumb the psychological depths its premise hints at, Reborn (also titled Deadly Doll) offers instead a superficial portrait of loss that is neither scary nor unsettling. Read the full review
28. True Love, for Once in My Life

Picture this: you are a middle-aged Hong Kong woman married to your first love, with whom you share two young-adult children, and you learn that your husband has fallen for another woman during his long business trips in Guangzhou, China. What will you do?
For executive producer Sabrina Tse Shuk-fun, it is the perfect opportunity to turn her unfaithful partner into a perpetual reminder in her life.
A self-financed autobiographical story, this film about a wife’s devotion to a cheating ex-husband is as awkward and self-indulgent as it was always going to be. It is curious to see True Love, for Once in My Life secure a commercial release while still being so raw around the edges. Read the full review
27. A Gilded Game

The dehumanising tendency of corporate finance and the merciless nature of stock market speculation serve as the basis for much of the drama in A Gilded Game, a mildly diverting film presumably made for viewers who will not mind its predictably moralistic lecturing tone.
There is minimal subtlety to Herman Yau Lai-to’s tale of market manipulation – and he is not the slightest bit embarrassed by it.
The rise and fall of an innocent newcomer in a profession that proves alternately lucrative and deadly is a story often told, because it works. What A Gilded Game lacks, however, is at least a couple of engaging characters with believable motivations. Read the full review
26. Road to Vendetta

As one of the most handsome faces to emerge from Hong Kong’s latest generation of film actors, Jeffrey Ngai Tsun-sang’s ascent to a leading role was inevitable. After playing several supporting parts, he is finally getting the chance.
Road to Vendetta, a violent, John Wick-inspired action thriller co-produced by Hong Kong and Japan, delivers as a pop idol vehicle with its ample visual style. For all the effort to make an anti-hero out of Ngai, however, the film’s screenplay appears multiple revisions away from telling a convincing story.
Road to Vendetta is a textbook case of style over substance. Read the full review
25. My First of May

In My First of May, a former star athlete sidelined by an old career-ending injury must emerge from his despair to care for the disabled teenage daughter he has long ignored.
This gentle effort by James Hung Ling-ching (The Seventh Lie), the latest in a long line of Hong Kong films in the past decade to look to home for bittersweet sentiment, is a warm-hearted but clichéd family drama that makes little attempt to conceal its calculated moves to wring tears from its viewers.
The writer-director has his trio of leading performers – Aaron Kwok Fu-shing, Natalie Hsu En-yi and Nina Paw Hee-ching – to thank for keeping the paper-thin plot watchable. Read the full review
24. Little Red Sweet

If you are looking for a new film that epitomises some of the trends prevalent in Hong Kong cinema and offers a taste of its current state, you cannot go too far wrong with Little Red Sweet.
This well-meaning family drama – the feature debut of director Vincent Chow Wing – tackles the familiar themes of generational conflict and urban redevelopment. It also features nostalgia for a home-grown food recipe and a passing thought about emigration.
Despite a tender atmosphere that is embraced by its reliable cast, Little Red Sweet is a little stale in flavour and somewhat undercooked in its dramatic expression. Its peddling of wistful sentiments towards traditional Hong Kong culture is a calculated move, but one not fully followed through. Read the full review
23. The Dumpling Queen

The inspiring true story behind the founding of an iconic Hong Kong food brand receives a largely uninspired retelling in The Dumpling Queen, a glossy if unabashedly melodramatic and run-of-the-mill biopic set primarily in the city in the late 1970s and 80s.
The film represents a rare return to Hong Kong-centric stories for veteran filmmaker Andrew Lau Wai-keung. It tells the rags-to-riches story of Wanchai Ferry founder Zang Jianhe (or Chong Kin-wo), played by Chinese comic actress Ma Li without even a hint of humour.
Lau’s version of events is so sanitised and agreeably sentimental that it sometimes feels like you are reading Wanchai Ferry’s brand story from a company brochure. Read the full review
22. Good Game

The intense, jargon-filled world of professional esports might seem like a rather esoteric setting for a film. Yet in the comedy-drama Good Game, this competitive backdrop is used for something unexpectedly heartwarming: a simple underdog story about chasing dreams and overcoming obstacles.
Director Dickson Leung Kwok-fai’s follow-up to his goofy fantasy comedy Yum Investigation is defined less by its geeky premise than its undemanding nature and unabashedly life-affirming spirit – and is far more interested in the heart of its players than in winning at a video game.
While the decision to shoot the gameplay scenes in live action is a refreshing way to convey the virtual experience, Good Game is otherwise too conceptually lightweight and artificially inspirational in its telling of the players’ personal stories, often leaning on sentimentality over substance. Read the full review
21. Under Current

Under Current is a quintessential example of the Hong Kong crime film in its present, transitional state. While it paints a mostly watchable portrait of greed and corruption, the film’s story also happens to be thoroughly detached from reality.
Directed by Alan Mak Siu-fai (Infernal Affairs) from a screenplay he co-scripted with Lam Fung, this thematic follow-up to Mak’s 2019 anti-corruption drama Integrity again takes a distinctly convoluted narrative to ponder the state of law enforcement, only to lose much of its conviction with preposterous twists and turns.
Ultimately, this most superficial account of justice and conscience is only leavened by its occasional action scenes and few stand-out supporting characters. Read the full review
20. Remember What I Forgot

The eccentric behaviour of an excessively passionate Hong Kong film geek serves as the inspiration behind Remember What I Forgot, a sentimental comedy-drama set on the fringes of the city’s fabled entertainment industry.
Shot in 2017 but shelved until this year, this directorial debut from Keian Chui Tze-yiu – whose second film, Showbiz Spy, was released in 2021 – gently amuses with its observations on some rarely considered aspects of Hong Kong cinema, only to fumble the plot whenever it shifts its focus onto the personal life of its fictionalised protagonist. Read the full review
19. Peg O’ My Heart

Nick Cheung Ka-fai is one of the most prominent active Hong Kong actors to have dabbled in directing, and in doing so has proved himself a talented visual stylist and world builder with a penchant for the dark and supernatural.
But the main reason Cheung is not known as a visionary filmmaker is that, despite all his stylish visuals, he is a clumsy storyteller who could not fashion a convincing narrative to save his life.
For much of its first half, Peg O’ My Heart intrigues with a barrage of sometimes spooky, often surreal images from its characters’ worst nightmares. For viewers who remain engaged enough to care about the central mystery, however, disappointment beckons. Read the full review
18. Valley of the Shadow of Death

Can a pastor forgive the young man who raped his daughter and indirectly caused her death? That is the premise of Valley of the Shadow of Death, a bold yet deeply flawed attempt to rethink Christianity’s advocacy of unconditional kindness.
Austere in atmosphere and exceptionally narrow in narrative scope, this portrait of a spiritual void is thought-provoking enough at first. It then avoids the toughest question that it has posed: should a devout Christian be expected to forgive the enemy just because that is the way?
The film sets the stage beautifully for this age-old dilemma to be reconsidered, only to shy away from providing an answer and drown the question out with distracting sideshows. Read the full review
17. Smashing Frank

A quartet of rookie robbers take on the elite yet corrupt members of the older generation in Smashing Frank. This stylish but lightweight crime drama, however, is considerably more effective as a portrait of disaffected youth than as a parable on the growing chasm between young and old, and wealthy and poor in Hong Kong.
Writer-director Trevor Choi Hong-ying’s ambivalent characterisation of the protagonists ultimately paints a more muddled picture of what his message truly is.
Viewers are far more likely to recall Hedwig Tam Sin-yin’s chain-smoking demeanour than anything her lead character has to say about the deeply entrenched generational conflict in contemporary Hong Kong. Read the full review
16. Golden Boy

A mildly entertaining boxing drama anchored by an eye-catching central performance, Golden Boy was first earmarked to kick-start the then-stuttering career of Louis Cheung Kai-chung – only to stay in post-production limbo for years.
As in most respectable boxing films that the city has recently produced, it features a past-his-prime boxer who finds redemption in the ring against much younger opponents, partly for the child that somehow ends up in his care.
Directed by TVB veteran Joe Chan Wai-kwan with a focus on brash humour rather than subtle emotions, this hot-blooded but generic tale about perseverance works better as a record of Cheung’s sheer determination to reinvent himself than as the artistic breakout that he had hoped it would be. Read the full review
15. TBA
This review will be added at a later date.
14. Atonement

Ronald Cheng Chung-kei sets aside his laid-back comedic persona for an utterly grim role in this devastating Thailand-set action thriller, which sees the Hong Kong singer and actor buff up and play an anguished father on a deadly hunt for the evil locals who have taken his young daughter.
A co-directing effort by Cheng and Mark Wu Yiu-fai – Cheng’s director and co-star in the 2015 buddy comedy Undercover Duet – Atonement’s clunky storytelling at times makes one wonder how a more sophisticated director could make a better film out of the same materials.
Ultimately, however, this powerful tale of a father who would do anything to avenge his daughter proves a thoroughly engaging watch in spite of its flaws. Read the full review
13. Montages of a Modern Motherhood

It is all doom and gloom for the first-time mother at the heart of Montages of a Modern Motherhood, a meticulously observed and wonderfully acted drama, but one that may not connect with a wider audience because of its unusual and almost perverse resolve to paint new parenthood as a completely joyless experience.
It marks the much-anticipated return of writer-director Oliver Chan Siu-kuen, whose 2019 hit Still Human is regarded by many as one of the best first features by a Hong Kong filmmaker in the past decade. Yet the new film could not be more different in tone from her heartwarming debut.
Hedwig Tam’s nuanced performance is the best part of this realistic tale of maternity in a patriarchal Chinese society. It is a pity to see her effort diluted by the lack of interesting characters around her – as well as by Chan’s single-minded, borderline monotonous determination to arrive at the worst-case scenario, if only to prove her point. Read the full review
12. The Remnant

Philip Keung finds just the right mix of menace and humanity in The Remnant, a gloomy drama with an obligatory sense of hope. He plays Tai, a former gang boss who, after a prison sentence for a deadly turf war, lives a quiet and solitary life in an old district as the owner of a traditional laundromat.
The film draws from a well of familiar traits: the nostalgic portrayal of triad brotherhood, the championing of chivalry, a commentary on generational conflict, and one of Hong Kong cinema’s most recurrent plotlines – the villainous efforts of property developers to evict tenants from their homes.
This feature debut by documentary filmmaker Kwan Man-hin is a largely compelling watch, its uneven pacing offset by a narrative that takes fairly surprising turns. Read the full review
11. Someone Like Me

Even with just two films under her belt, Tam Wai-ching may already be the closest thing to an emerging auteur that Hong Kong cinema has to offer – for better or for worse.
Unlike her first film, In Your Dreams, Someone Like Me initially appears to align with the focus on social issues prevalent in recent Hong Kong films. However, this turns out to be the least of Tam’s concerns as her story unfolds; the film turns its awkward premise on its head and into an intimate character study.
Acting accolades may naturally follow Fish Liew Chi-yu in the coming awards season, for her challenging role as a woman with cerebral palsy who wishes to experience sex. Read the full review
10. Behind the Shadows

Louis Koo Tin-lok, the Hong Kong film industry’s most prolific star and arguably its most influential investor too, puts his signature blend of deadpan charisma and dramatic intensity to fine use in Behind the Shadows, the first Malaysian production of his company One Cool Film.
A character-driven detective thriller that shows a fascination with cheating wives and jilted husbands, the film sees Koo play a hardbitten private investigator who, while running away from his own marriage, must track down a serial killer who seems to bear a grudge against unfaithful women.
Surprisingly for a film whose first half is so well scripted, Behind the Shadows reveals the killer’s identity at its halfway point. After this, unfortunately, neither the motive of the perpetrator nor the ensuing cat-and-mouse game elevate the film beyond a conventional romp. Read the full review
9. My Best Bet

A woman with a severe gambling addiction marries a man with an over-the-top aversion to people who gamble in this Lunar New Year comedy from Andy Lo Yiu-fai.
Lo shows a gift for nonsensical humour after taking a more sophisticated approach to contemplating family ties in his first two films, Happiness and Once in a Blue Moon.
The writer-director’s big achievement here lies in the way he subverts his audience’s expectations about the “good” and “bad” guys as if he has always recognised how pointless it is to rehash the formula of traditional gambling comedies. Read the full review
8. Hit N Fun

A year after he earned much acclaim for the character-driven crime drama Rob N Roll, director Albert Mak Kai-kwong is back for another crack at the Lunar New Year box office with this unusually warm-hearted boxing drama.
Hit N Fun tells a bittersweet and at times almost philosophical story that again feels like it is striving to separate itself from all those Lunar New Year comedies. Its few moments of slapstick humour aside, this is a largely sombre tale in which characters learn to accept their lot.
The film is not the hot-blooded sports drama about overcoming the odds that viewers might have expected, but there is no denying this is a product of its time – and a rather engaging one at that. Read the full review
7. Fight for Tomorrow

A potentially corny tale involving old-fashioned gangland heroics and an ex-convict father’s belated redemption is told with a great deal of nuance and heart in Fight for Tomorrow, a refreshingly uplifting crime drama by Hong Kong writer-director Chan Tai-lee (Tomorrow is Another Day).
In a rare leading role that marks his best performance in years, veteran actor Patrick Tam Yiu-man leaves a vivid impression as Shek Sam-long, a former mob boss who was once known for his ferocious attitude, but has since lost both his marriage and fortune after several years in prison.
Chan’s endeavour to weave in the pandemic – it was written in 2019 and shot in late 2022 – and pitch Sam-long’s struggles to hold on to his former glory as a parable for Hong Kong is flawed but ultimately rewarding. Read the full review
6. Pass and Goal

The prospect of Pass and Goal is not immediately appealing. A football-themed comedy with an old-fashioned premise about staying true to oneself, it features a modest cast that includes several inexperienced child actors and is directed by the filmmaker behind abysmal comedies like Lucky Fat Man (2017) and Love Detective (2015).
Yet, to echo a key message from Jil Wong Pak-kei’s latest film, a match is seldom won or lost in the first half. And so it proves here, as this feel-good, family-friendly offering defies expectations with its infectious positivity and cheeky humour.
An inspirational underdog story that makes no attempt to hide its melodramatic calculations, Pass and Goal relies on the natural chemistry of its ensemble cast and screenwriter Ambrose Kwok Yat-choi’s funny dialogue to deliver a thoroughly enjoyable time for all the family. Read the full review
5. Vital Signs

In Vital Signs, the converging career paths of two ambulance paramedics at very different stages in their lives provide the unusual backdrop for a soul-searching drama rooted in today’s Hong Kong, with characters pondering the essence of home as they reluctantly face a wave of emigration from the city.
The fourth feature by writer-director Cheuk Wan-chi and her first since the 2014 comedy Temporary Family, Vital Signs comes across as a notably more mature work by the multi-hyphenate who is also known as a stand-up comedian – even if its themes are not always as tightly woven as they could be.
Viewers expecting an exciting rescue drama might be underwhelmed by the dearth of action in Vital Signs. Whatever her film lacks in genre thrills, Cheuk more than makes up for with sensitive character drama. Read the full review
4. She’s Got No Name

An unsolved real-life murder in Japanese-occupied Shanghai in 1945 provides the backdrop for a vicious battle of the sexes in She’s Got No Name. Peter Chan Ho-sun’s captivating period drama proves both morally ambiguous and thematically diffuse – though perhaps not entirely by its filmmakers’ design.
This first half of a two-part film follows Zhang Ziyi’s unnamed killer in the brief period between her arrest for the murder and dismemberment of her husband (Wang Chuanjun) and her dramatic change in fortune right at the end of World War II.
With the coming part two, whether Chan can turn this thrilling, if clichéd, drama about archaic patriarchal order into a more resounding whole remains to be seen. Read the full review
3. Sons of the Neon Night

Few Hong Kong films have arrived with as much anticipation as Juno Mak Chun-lung’s Sons of the Neon Night, a hyper-stylised crime thriller with a reported HK$400 million (US$51.4 million) budget and an unusually star-studded cast.
To focus on the convoluted, nearly kaleidoscopic story – set in an alternate-reality Hong Kong of 1994 – and its many loose ends would be to miss the pleasures of this ambitious effort, which oozes dark humour in its often cynical dialogue and boasts spectacular production design that renders every location a little surreal.
This is the work of an artist fully committed to the dark, lawless world he has created – a rarity in today’s Hong Kong cinema.
Ultimately, Sons of the Neon Night is best viewed not as a coherent narrative where closure is a goal, but as the uncompromisingly vivid fever dream of a crime film buff with a great eye for visual design. Read the full review
2. Another World

Hong Kong filmmakers rarely get as philosophical about the human condition as they do in the animated feature Another World, which contemplates the limits of goodness in the face of great evil, set against a vibrant action fantasy backdrop.
Adapted from Naka Saijo’s novel Sennenki: Thousand-Year Journey of an Oni with both narrative flair and visual potency, this impressive effort by first-time director Tommy Ng Kai-chung and writer-producer Polly Yeung Po-man is an anomaly in more ways than one.
Although the film’s focus on reincarnation appears to align it with Eastern religions, Another World’s belief in kindness renders it a universally engaging watch.
The film is bound to be seen as a milestone for the city’s animation industry – perhaps ironically, given that there is very little on screen to indicate its Hong Kong roots. Read the full review
1. The Way We Talk

In his six films to date, Hong Kong writer-director Adam Wong Sau-ping has repeatedly revealed a soft spot for protagonists who are trying to understand themselves and find their place in the world.
But he has not come close to matching the immersive sense of soul-searching he evokes in The Way We Talk. This deeply humane drama about a trio of young deaf people is Wong’s best film yet by some distance.
A beautifully engrossing tale of self-discovery set around a complex topic of contention, the film makes a valiant effort to simulate the experience of hearing loss, incorporating both meticulous sound design and long, wordless sequences that transcend the formula of mainstream filmmaking.
The unassuming Chung Suet-ying impresses not just with her sensitive performance but also her convincing deaf accent and eloquence in sign language. Read the full review
For our ranking of the Hong Kong films released in 2024, read here.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Thailand rediscovers ‘extinct’ flat-headed cat after 30 years
data.description:
An elusive wild cat long feared extinct in Thailand has been rediscovered three decades after the last recorded sighting, conservation authorities and an NGO said on Friday.
Flat-headed cats are among the world’s rarest and most threatened wild felines. Their range is limited to Southeast Asia and they are endangered because of dwindling habitat.
The domestic cat-sized feline with its distinctive round and close-set eyes was last spotted in a documented sighting in Thailand in 1995.
But an ecological survey that began last year, using camera traps in southern Thailand’s Princess Sirindhorn Wildlife Sanctuary, documented 29 sightings, according to the country’s Department of National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation and wild cat conservation organisation Panthera.
“The rediscovery is exciting, yet concerning at the same time,” said veterinarian and researcher Kaset Sutasha of Kasetsart University, noting that habitat fragmentation has left the species increasingly “isolated”.
It is unclear exactly how many cats were photographed, as the species lacks the markings needed to tell them apart.
But the findings suggest a relatively high concentration of the species, said Panthera conservation programme manager Rattapan Pattanarangsan.
The footage included a female flat-headed cat with her cub – a rare and encouraging sign for a species that typically produces only one offspring at a time.

Nocturnal and elusive, the flat-headed cat typically lives in dense wetland ecosystems such as peat swamps and freshwater mangroves, environments that are extremely difficult for researchers to access, Rattapan said.
Globally, the International Union for Conservation of Nature estimates that around 2,500 adult flat-headed cats remain in the wild, classifying the species as endangered.
In Thailand, it has long been listed as “possibly extinct”.
Thailand’s peat swamp forests have been heavily fragmented, largely due to land conversion and agricultural expansion, said Kaset, who was not involved in the ecological survey but has researched wild cats for years.
The animals also face mounting threats from disease spread by domestic animals, and they struggle to reproduce across isolated areas.
While the rediscovery offers hope, it is only a “starting point” for future conservation efforts, he said.
“What comes after this is more important – how to enable them to live alongside us sustainably, without being threatened.”

data.title: China aircraft carrier allows use of naked flames for TCM therapy which is popular among troops
data.description:
China’s most advanced aircraft carrier, the Fujian, has allowed the use of naked flames on board to accommodate a form of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM).
The use of fire on the vessel is to be allowed so that cupping therapy can take place.
The news has received much attention on mainland social media after it was reported by state broadcaster CCTV on December 24.

The Fujian, China’s third aircraft carrier and the most advanced, officially entered into service in November at a grand ceremony in Sanya, Hainan province.
With a carrying capacity of 1,000 sailors, the Fujian is equipped with the biggest medical centre in the country’s naval fleet.
It is made up of a diagnosis zone, inpatient department, surgery rooms and a physiotherapy room where the TCM treatments moxibustion, cupping and acupuncture take place.
Cupping therapy is an ancient healing technique to ease pain.

The treatment provider first heats the inside of each cup by setting an alcohol-soaked cotton ball aflame, so that heat sends oxygen out of the cup. Then the cups are quickly placed on specific parts of the patient’s body, like the back, stomach, arms and legs, where they stay for some minutes.
The rationale behind the treatment is to use a vacuum or suction force inside the cup to draw blood to or away from specific areas of the body to relieve conditions that cause pain.
According to the military authorities, the flames are “necessary fire”, the report said.

“It plays a definite role in the rehabilitation of sailors and is used in a strictly controlled manner,” a military official was quoted as saying.
The cupping treatment is practised for a short time, on a small scale and by professionals.
Also, the medical centre is installed with fireproof materials and an independent ventilation system.
The use of flames is prohibited in on board kitchens.
Cupping and other TCM physiotherapies are popular among military personnel because soldiers tend to develop problems such as back and neck pain or shoulder stiffness in the wet environment.
The Fujian enforces a strict fire prevention code.
Only electric stoves are used in its kitchen and sailors’ quilts are made of flame-resistant fabrics.

“We attach high importance to fire use on the aircraft carrier because it is seen as ‘a movable ammunition depot’ and we learn lessons from multiple previous fire accidents,” the official said, referring to an incident aboard the US carrier Lexington and Britain’s Dasher decades ago.
“Food can be prepared using electricity, but the health of sailors cannot be ensured with modern technology,” CCTV said in an editorial.
It is not the first time cupping treatment has made international headlines.
American swimming superstar Michael Phelps stunned the public with a raft of marks on his back left by cupping therapy when he took part in the 2016 Olympics.

data.title: China opens shopping centre on Woody Island in disputed Paracels in South China Sea
data.description:
China has opened a new shopping centre on Woody Island in the disputed Paracel Islands, bolstering its civilian presence on the strategically vital South China Sea outpost.
The Sansha City Commercial Centre opened to business on Thursday, according to the local government. The over 6,000 square metre (64,600 sq ft) complex is the latest addition to China’s civilian infrastructure on what it calls Yongxing Island, following its first hardware store last year and a hotpot restaurant in 2023.
Woody Island lies within the Paracel Islands – an archipelago known as the Xisha Islands in Chinese that is at the centre of overlapping territorial claims in the South China Sea.
Woody Island is the largest of around 30 islands in the Paracels chain and serves as the administrative hub of Sansha, a city established in Hainan province in 2012 to assert Beijing’s territorial claims in the South China Sea.

Sansha governs the Paracels, as well as the disputed Spratly Islands and Macclesfield Bank, called Nansha Islands and Zhongsha Islands, respectively, in China.
The city had a permanent population of about 2,200 at the end of 2024, according to data from the Hainan statistics bureau.
China has already built a host of public infrastructure on Woody Island, including a post office, bank, weather station, school, library, hospital, power plant and public parks.
It also has a dual-use civilian and military airport with a 3,000-metre runway capable of accommodating Boeing 737 aircraft, as well as a port able to berth 5,000-tonne vessels.
The new commercial centre combines shopping, dining, cultural and leisure facilities, according to the Sansha government, which described it as a step towards improving the living standards for residents of Woody Island as well as civilian and military personnel stationed there.
The facilities included a supermarket, a fast-food restaurant, a bookstore and a hair salon, officials said.

Beijing’s continued expansion of civilian infrastructure on disputed islands in the South China Sea has prompted concerns from rival claimant Vietnam.
In 2023, Hanoi strongly denounced the opening of the hotpot restaurant on Woody Island, reiterating its position that such activities undermined its claimed sovereignty over the Paracels, which it calls the Hoang Sa Islands.
Beijing claims most South China Sea features and their adjacent waters, but those claims are disputed by neighbours including Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia and Brunei.
Over more than a decade, Beijing has steadily consolidated its South China Sea presence through a mix of administrative control, infrastructure development and military deployment, often prompting protests from rival claimants.
In 2016, an international arbitration ruling initiated by the Philippines under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea invalidated China’s expansive maritime claims as having no legal basis. Beijing boycotted the proceedings and rejected the outcome.

data.title: Shanghai to double down on open-source projects amid push for AI, chip self-sufficiency
data.description:
Shanghai, China’s financial and tech hub, is aiming to foster over 200 open-source projects over the next two years as part of the city’s ambitious road map to build a world-class ecosystem amid the heated AI race with the US.
By 2027, the city would see the establishment of one to two “internationally influential open-source communities”, along with 100 companies focused on related technologies and over 200 high-quality open-source projects, according to an action plan unveiled on Thursday by the Shanghai municipal government.
The total number of developers involved in the open-source communities is expected to reach over three million by that time, it added.
Several frontier sectors – including AI, intelligent chips and high-end software – would become major areas of focus for the initiative. The city also planned to launch China’s first open-source platform targeting overseas markets next year.
The ambitious plan was put forward by a few municipal agencies, led by the Shanghai Municipal Commission of Economy and Informatization, the body in charge of boosting technology development in the city.

The initiative, which aims to make Shanghai “a nationally competitive source and development hub for open-source innovation”, reflects how the city is positioning itself as the vanguard of China’s push for tech self-reliance.
Shanghai is headquarters for a number of AI model developers, including MiniMax and SenseTime, as well as AI chip developers such as Biren Technology and Enflame.
The latest aspirations from Shanghai come as the Chinese government is doubling down on its open-source strategy, especially in AI, to cut reliance on US technologies and foster domestic innovation amid the US-China tech war.
The Shanghai municipal government said it would boost open-source platforms with resources such as pre-trained models, training data sets, development tools and AI applications, to meet the needs of the entire chain from development to training, testing, hosting and operation for AI models.
China’s bet on open-source models has already helped it become a key player in the global AI arena, led by the efforts by start-up DeepSeek and Alibaba Group Holding. Open source allows public access to a software program’s source code, allowing third-party developers to modify or share its design, fix broken links or scale up its capabilities. Alibaba owns the Post.

China’s open-source AI models may have caught up or “even pulled ahead” of their US counterparts in capabilities and adoption, according to a recent report by Stanford University. These “good enough” models with higher degrees of “openness” compared with US models offered practical advantages such as reproducibility and efficiency, especially in less computationally resourced parts of the world, it added.
As part of its open-source plan, Shanghai will also make efforts to push forward the adoption of RISC-V chip architecture, with the goal of building related toolchains covering compilers, simulators and operating system adaptation tools.
RISC-V, the fifth generation of the open-sourced Reduced Instruction Set Computer architecture for processors, is free for anyone to use and modify and has been seen as one of China’s best hopes for achieving chip self-sufficiency.

data.title: Hong Kong Jockey Club plans fourth public riding school in Tseung Kwan O
data.description:
The Hong Kong Jockey Club is planning to build its fourth public riding school on a former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O to reduce applicant wait times and boost the equestrian talent pool.
The project has already gained in-principle support from authorities and the club will seek town planning approval in the first quarter of next year and begin construction as early as the end of 2027, according to a paper submitted to Sai Kung District Council.
The plan will be part of the agenda of a council meeting on January 6.
The facility is expected to be open to the public in 2030 to address strong demand, with more than 20,000 people currently queuing for a spot in the existing three schools and average waiting times lasting several years.
“To cater for the growing demand, the club has decided to open its fourth public riding school in Tseung Kwan O after cautious planning,” the paper read.
“Apart from facilitating residents living in the southeastern New Territories, we also hope to shorten the overall waiting time and nurture more excellent equestrians.
“The proposal can utilise the restored landfill site while promoting Hong Kong’s equestrian sport development. The proposal kills two birds with one stone.”
The club has three public riding schools in Tuen Mun in the New Territories, and Pok Fu Lam and Lei Yue Mun Park on Hong Kong Island.
According to the club’s proposal, the proposed school will take up 46,500 square metres of the former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O.
The school will comprise three low-rise blocks, which will incorporate accessibility design features.
Apart from accommodating equine facilities, such as paddocks and stables, the school will also feature a viewing gallery for the public to look at the training and appreciate the natural scenery.
A pony observation area will be earmarked for the public to get a closer look at the animals and take photos with them, offering a unique experience for visitors.
Upon operation, public open days, regular visits and pony rides will be offered as public education activities.
The club said construction would not involve piling works, while it would also urge contractors to mitigate the impact on nearby residents and the environment.
It added that it would implement a temporary transport management based on the government’s request.
Citing its consultant’s preliminary transport assessment report, the club said the fourth riding school would only slightly increase the district’s vehicle and passenger flow upon operation.
Closed in 1994, the former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O development area 105 has a total of 42 hectares. It is currently used as a training field for unmanned aerial vehicles for land surveying.
In 2005, the site was developed into a model aeroplane training field for the Hong Kong Air Cadet Corps during weekends and public holidays.
